<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141526 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00632: Interruptible activity region

- page_id: `189141526`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141526/Interruptible+activity+region
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:20.317+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interruptable region contains activity nodes. When a token leaves an interruptable region via edges designated by the region as interrupting edges, all tokens and behaviours in the region are terminated.

Specify the Interruptible Activity Region in the Interruptible Activity Region [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''].

To make an edge as interrupting, simply select an edge and click the "lightning" button, as shown in the image bellow.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Mark edge as interrupting

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Interruptable region contains activity nodes. When a token leaves an interruptable region via edges designated by the region as interrupting edges, all tokens and behaviours in the region are terminated.</p><p>Specify the Interruptible Activity Region in the Interruptible Activity Region <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</p><p>To make an edge as interrupting, simply select an edge and click the &quot;lightning&quot; button, as shown in the image bellow.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="make_interrupting.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>Mark edge as interrupting</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145874 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00633: Introducing Macro Engine

- page_id: `189145874`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145874/Introducing+Macro+Engine
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:46:21.690+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Macro Engine (previously called Script Engine) allows you to create your own macro (script) by using BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript Rhino, or Jython. With Macro Engine, you can control everything that is allowed in Open API, for example, transforming and manipulating models. Learn more about Open API in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Developer Guides 2024xR2 Home' space='DEVG2024xR2'] and find sample macros in *<modeling tool installation directory>/samples/product features/macros*.

Macro Engine comes with Professional, Architect, and Enterprise Editions starting from version 16.6 and later.

##### Changing Javascript Nashorn to Javascript Rhino

The Macro Engine supports Javascript Rhino. If you have any macros written in Javascript Nashorn, which is [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn' space=''], you can change it to Javascript Rhino.

To change to Javascript Rhino

- Click Tools > Macros > Change JS Nashorn to Rhino

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Macro Engine (previously called Script Engine) allows you to create your own macro (script) by using BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript Rhino, or Jython. With Macro Engine, you can control everything that is allowed in Open API, for example, transforming and manipulating models. Learn more about Open API in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Developer Guides 2024xR2 Home" ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Developer Guide]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and find sample macros in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;/samples/product features/macros</em>.</p><p>Macro Engine comes with Professional, Architect, and Enterprise Editions starting from version 16.6 and later.</p><h3>Changing Javascript Nashorn to Javascript Rhino</h3><p>The Macro Engine supports Javascript Rhino. If you have any macros written in Javascript Nashorn, which is <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[deprecated]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, you can change it to Javascript Rhino.</p><p>To change to Javascript Rhino</p><hr /><ul><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Macros</strong> &gt; <strong>Change JS Nashorn to Rhino</strong></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145032 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00634: Introduction

- page_id: `189145032`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145032/Introduction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:24.666+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1355068225

1355068226

1355068226

This user guide demonstrates how to create a report template using Report Wizard and use it in the modeling tool. To make the most of Report Wizard, you need to understand Apache Velocity and the modeling tool elements. Report Wizard uses Apache Velocity, this means that you will need to know how to create a Velocity template and that every rule that applies to Velocity will also apply to the Report Wizard.

Velocity is a Java-based template engine that processes templates and references Java objects to produce output documents. A basic Velocity template can contain static text, layouts, conditional statements, and placeholders for each referenced Java object. When a template is being read by Velocity, conditional statements will be processed and placeholders will be replaced with the value from the referenced Java objects. You can use Velocity, for example, to generate web pages, emails, and read *XML* documents, but with Report Wizard it is now possible to use Velocity to read other types of templates, such as Rich Text Format documents. This user guide covers some basic aspects of Velocity, modeling tool elements, and Report Wizard directives.

1355068224

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

To make the most of Report Wizard, you need to understand Apache Velocity and the modeling tool elements. Report Wizard uses Apache Velocity, this means that you will need to know how to create a Velocity template and that every rule that applies to Velocity will also apply to the Report Wizard.</p><p>Velocity is a Java-based template engine that processes templates and references Java objects to produce output documents. A basic Velocity template can contain static text, layouts, conditional statements, and placeholders for each referenced Java object. When a template is being read by Velocity, conditional statements will be processed and placeholders will be replaced with the value from the referenced Java objects. You can use Velocity, for example, to generate web pages, emails, and read <em>XML</em> documents, but with Report Wizard it is now possible to use Velocity to read other types of templates, such as Rich Text Format documents. This user guide covers some basic aspects of Velocity, modeling tool elements, and Report Wizard directives.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2794887a-6f98-48f2-9b85-a3f47c2851b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068224</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="4dc280e8-5e54-4745-a5b5-4e96822291c6" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189143316 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00637: Invalid Redefinition

- page_id: `189143316`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189143316/Invalid+Redefinition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:40:03.717+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > UML validation rules > UML correctness constraints rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvRdf****

**Description**

This validation rule checks if a property redefines the inherited property of the owner classifier.

**Severity**

Error

**Constrained Element**

Property, Operation

**Solvers**

To fix this, do one of the following:

- Change the Redefined Property value to the appropriate one that is inherited, not owned.
- Remove Element solver - removes the invalid redefining property from the model.

**Example**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The element *Car* owns two attributes, *wheel* and *type*. The attribute*wheel*redefines the attribute *type*, causing the validation error. 
The error is resolved by changing the *wheel*attribute's **Redefined Property**value to the appropriate one, which is the *wheel*attribute, inherited from the element *Transport*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvRdf<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if a property redefines the inherited property of the owner classifier.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>Error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Property, Operation</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><p>To fix this, do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Change the <strong>Redefined Property </strong>value to the appropriate one that is inherited, not owned.</li><li><strong>Remove Element</strong> solver -  removes the invalid redefining property from the model.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="invalid_redefinition.png" /></ac:image><br /><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The element <em>Car</em> owns two attributes, <em>wheel</em> and <em>type</em>. The attribute </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);"><em>wheel</em> </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">redefines the attribute <em>type</em>, causing the validation error.<br />The error is resolved by changing the <em>wheel </em>attribute's <strong>Redefined Property </strong>value to the appropriate one, which is the <em>wheel </em>attribute, inherited from the element <em>Transport</em></span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">.</span></h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145614 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00640: Java Annotation Member

- page_id: `189145614`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145614/Java+Annotation+Member
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:53.921+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Java annotation member is mapped directly to the UML Operation owned by the interface stereotyped as «JavaAnnotation». This operation, by itself, can have the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember». However, it is optional, unless specify a default value for it.

A Java annotation member type is mapped to a UML Type property of a UML Parameter with the “Return“ direction kind.

Annotation member mapping table:

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Annotation member declaration | UML Operation with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember» (Optional), owned by a UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». |
| Annotation member name | UML Operation Name |
| Annotation member documentation | UML Operation Documentation |
| Annotation member type | Mapped to the UML Parameter type with the “return“ direction kind (resides in UML Operation parameters list) |
| Annotation member type modifier | Mapped to the property “Type Modifier“ |
| Default value | {JavaAnnotationMemberDefaultValue} tagged value of a «JavaAnnotationMember» stereotype. The stereotype is set on UML Operation |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

UML model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UML Interface representing Java annotation type with Java annotation members

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

This operation, by itself, can have the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember». The stereotype is set on UML Operation</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6222f7c8-85f8-4edc-9875-549d33a27403"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb1c4bf0-261c-43a5-926c-ad1a3bf3a32e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[/**

## PAGE 00641: Java Annotation Type

- page_id: `189145612`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

A Java annotation declaration is mapped directly to the UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». Annotation modifiers are mapped into UML Interface properties. Annotation members are mapped to the UML Interface operations with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember».

Annotation mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Annotation declaration | UML Interface with stereotype «JavaAnnotation» (optional) |
| Annotation name | UML Interface name |
| Annotation documentation | UML Interface Documentation |
| Visibility modifier | UML Interface "Visibility" property |


## PAGE 00642: Java Annotations Usage

- page_id: `189145616`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145616/Java+Annotations+Usage

- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431991466

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431991468

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431991467

You can annotate Java elements. This annotation is mapped on top of the {JavaAnnotations} tagged value of the stereotype «JavaElement». «JavaElement» is a base stereotype for all stereotypes used in Java mapping, and can be used directly or with any other stereotype derived from it. Annotation is mapped as a simple string value.

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code****

```java

```

Annotation is mapped as a simple string value.</span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e6db0d0-9da5-43cb-b723-2b1292f810d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code<strong> </strong></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="18651406-ca61-421f-a33d-e8b01c04c988"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class Test

````html
id = 2,
name = "Rick"
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145621 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00643: Java CE Options

- page_id: `189145621`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145621/Java+CE+Options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:54.997+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Java CE options are visible every time you attempt to reverse source code. This section describes the **Resolve collection generic**option for Java-specific options. Other options are common for all languages and are described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reverse Options' space=''].

##### Resolve collection generics option

By default, this option is selected in the **Reverse Options** dialog. Java introduced parameterized types in JLS 3 and added type variables to all Java collections. With reverse engineering, it is possible to find which type is in a Java collection and make associations directly to the contained type, instead of the Java collection.

Reverse engineering allows you to find a Java parameterized collection and retrieves the Java type used in a container. This type is set as a *Type* to the UML Property. The container type is set to the UML Property/Java language property “Container“ as a simple string.

#### TIP: Example

Example

**Source code sample**

```java
attribute; 
} ]]>
```

```text

```

UML Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Attribute type, retrieved from collection

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Collection type in UML Property specification

Note, that*$$type$$* shows where it should be in the lined UML Property type on code generation.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Java CE options are visible every time you attempt to reverse source code. This section describes the <strong>Resolve collection generic </strong>option for Java-specific options. Other options are common for all languages and are described in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Reverse Options" /></ac:link>.</p><h3>Resolve collection generics option</h3><p>By default, this option is selected in the <strong>Reverse Options</strong> dialog. Java introduced parameterized types in JLS 3 and added type variables to all Java collections. With reverse engineering, it is possible to find which type is in a Java collection and make associations directly to the contained type, instead of the Java collection.</p><p>Reverse engineering allows you to find a Java parameterized collection and retrieves the Java type used in a container. This type is set as a <em>Type</em> to the UML Property. The container type is set to the UML Property/Java language property “Container“ as a simple string.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="517c7ceb-087e-49e9-b601-303c1bb3e9f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Source code sample</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bd0fec67-0cd6-494d-8598-ea0278a183c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class Test 
{ 
    java.util.List<String> attribute; 
} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><pre><br /></pre><p>UML Model</p><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="69"><ri:attachment ri:filename="attribute_type_retrieved_from_collection.png" /></ac:image></p><h6><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Attribute type, retrieved from collection </span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="container_property.png" /></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Collection type in UML Property specification</h6><p>Note, that<em> $$type$$</em> shows where it should be in the lined UML Property type on code generation. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145601 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00644: Java Class

- page_id: `189145601`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145601/Java+Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:52.404+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431638635

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431638638

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431638636

A Java class is mapped directly to the UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='']with the stereotype «JavaClass». This stereotype is optional and if the UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java class. If no appropriate property is found in UML,class modifiers are mapped into UML Class properties or to the Java language properties for Class.

Java class fields, operations and inner classes are mapped to the appropriate UML Properties, UML Operations and UML Classes.

**Class mapping table**

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Class declaration | UML Class with stereotype «JavaClass» (optional) |
| Class name | UML Class name |
| Class Documentation | UML Class Documentation |
| Class extends Clause | Mapped to the UML Generalization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class |
| Class Implementation Clause | Mapped to the UML Interface Realization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class. |
| Visibility Modifier | UML Class “Visibility” property |
| Abstract Modifier | UML Class “Is Abstract” property |
| Final Modifier | UML Class “Is Final Specialization“ property |
| Static Modifier | Java Language property “Static modifier“ |
| Strictfp Modifier | Java Language property “Strictfp modifier“ |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

UML model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

This stereotype is optional and if the UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java class. <span>If no appropriate property is found in UML, </span>class modifiers are mapped into UML Class properties or to the Java language properties for Class.<br /></span></p><p><span>Java class fields, operations and inner classes are mapped to the appropriate UML Properties, UML Operations and class</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Class Implementation Clause</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the UML Interface Realization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Visibility Modifier</td><td><p><span>UML Class “Visibility” property</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Abstract Modifier</td><td><p><span>UML Class “Is Abstract” property</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Final Modifier</td><td><p><span>UML Class “Is Final Specialization“ property</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Static Modifier</td><td><span>Java Language property “Static modifier“</span></td></tr><tr><td>Strictfp Modifier</td><td><span>Java Language property “Strictfp modifier“</span></td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1be81bb5-b5e1-4f30-8a06-d3c627058e48"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a1723835-5b4a-4c83-8065-ffbb3d48b622"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[/**
````html
* Comment of the class MyList
*/
public final class MyList extends ArrayList implements Cloneable
{
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">UML model</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="class_diagram_ce_example.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fd6b691e-94b1-43ec-9ea1-922bdfad171b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431638634</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5225d784-dee8-466f-adeb-a20574349cca"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145592 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00645: Java Code Engineering

- page_id: `189145592`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145592/Java+Code+Engineering
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:50.539+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1295392607

1295392609

1295392608

This chapter describes how our modeling tool maps Java language elements to UML, helps choose which profiles to use, and describes Java code engineering properties.

This chapter features the following:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Mapping to UML' space=''] describes general rules our modeling tool uses to map each Java element to UML and which profile is used. You will find an example and corresponding model with marked properties used in Java to describe mapping rules.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java CE Options' space=''] introduces specific Java options.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Method Implementation Reverse' space=''] describes a functionality that switches Java from a Sequence Diagram to our modeling tool.

##### References

Gosling, J., Joy, B., Steele, G., Bracha, G., & Buckley, A. (2012, February 6). The JavaTM Language Specifica- tion. Java SE 7 Edition. Retrieved March 29, 2012, from [http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf)

##### Java support in modeling tools

You can perform the following actions:

- Import Java source code into a model (reverse engineering).
- Generate Java code from the model (code generation).
- Apply changes to the source code from the model (round-trip). You can change Java declaration headers and apply them to existing source code; however, you cannot change the method of implementation.
- Create a sequence diagram from the selected method body.
- Create a model from the Java byte code.

1295392606

**Related pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='']

You will find an example and corresponding model with marked properties used in Java to describe mapping rules.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ac:link> introduces specific Java options.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Method Implementation Reverse" /></ac:link> describes a functionality that switches Java from a Sequence Diagram to our modeling tool. </li></ol><h3><span>References</span></h3><p>Gosling, J., Joy, B., Steele, G., Bracha, G., &amp; Buckley, A. (2012, February 6). The JavaTM Language Specifica- tion. Java SE 7 Edition. Retrieved March 29, 2012, from <span style="color: rgb(90,109,148);"><a href="http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf">http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf</a></span></p><h3>Java support in modeling tools</h3><p>You can perform the following actions:</p><ul><li>Import Java source code into a model (reverse engineering).</li><li>Generate Java code from the model (code generation).</li><li>Apply changes to the source code from the model (round-trip). You can change Java declaration headers and apply them to existing source code; however, you cannot change the method of implementation.</li><li>Create a sequence diagram from the selected method body.</li><li>Create a model from the Java byte code. </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06e9267e-d31b-401b-b0fc-4296dac0206a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295392606</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong>:</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="e2e6db67-0699-46af-9992-06fee882a68a" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f267aa07-7072-427f-8ea2-77a199be81aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00646: Java Documentation Properties dialog box

- page_id: `189145587`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145587/Java+Documentation+Properties+dialog+box
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:49.803+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Global options for Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

419370658

419370660

419370659

To open the **Java Documentation Properties**dialog box

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='']**Project Options** dialog box .
2. In the Options list, expand Code Engineering and click Java Language Options .
3. In the Java Language Options area, select Java Doc as the Processor for Documentation .
4. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='three-dot-btn1.png'] to open the Documentation Properties dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Documentation Properties dialog box

| Box Name | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Tag Name | Type the tag name. |
| Value | Type the value of the tag. |
| Generate | The selected tag will be placed in the generated code as a comment before a classifier (class or interface), operation or attribute. |
| Up | Moves the selected item up the list. |
| Down | Moves the selected item down the list. |
| Add | Adds a new item in the list. |
| Remove | Removes the selected from the list. |
| OK | Saves changes and closes the dialog box. |
| Cancel | Closes the dialog box without saving changes. |
| Help | Displays help. |

419370657

## PAGE 00647: Java Enumeration

- page_id: `189145608`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145608/Java+Enumeration
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:53.204+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431929426

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431929428

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431929427

Java enumeration is mapped directly to the UML Class with the stereotype «JavaEnumeration». If no appropriate property is found in UML, enumeration modifiers are mapped into UML Class properties or to the Java language properties for the interface. Java enumeration literals are mapped to the UML Property with the stereotype «JavaEnumerationLiteral». All contained fields, operations, and inner classes are mapped to appropriate UML Properties, UML Operations, UML Classes. All mapping rules used in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Class' space=''] mapping apply to the Java enumeration.

#### TIP: Example

Java source code

```java

```

## PAGE 00648: Java Enumeration Literal

- page_id: `189145610`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145610/Java+Enumeration+Literal
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:53.445+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431945117

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431945119

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431945118

Java Enumeration Literal is mapped directly to the UML Property with the stereotype «JavaEnumerationLiteral». It does not require specific modifiers.

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Literal declaration | UML Parameter with stereotype «JavaEnumerationLiteral», owned by UML Class with stereotype «JavaEnumeration» |
| Literal name | UML Property Name |
| Literal documentation | When using JavaDoc preprocessing, it is mapped to the UML Parameter Documentation; otherwise, it is part of the UML Operation Documentation. |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145603 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00649: Java Field

- page_id: `189145603`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145603/Java+Field
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:52.608+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Examples

431872159

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Examples

431872162

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Examples

431872161

A Java field is mapped directly to the UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Attribute' space=''] with the stereotype «JavaField». This stereotype is optional and if a UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java field. If no appropriate property is found in UML, field modifiers are mapped into the UML Property properties or to the Java language properties.

Note that Java field type modifiers are mapped to the specific property *Type Modifier*, but not to the UML Multiplicity.

**Field mapping table**

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Field Declaration | UML Property, owned by UML Class, with stereotype «JavaProperty» (optional) |
| Field Name | UML Property Name |
| Field Documentation | UML Field Documentation |
| Field Type | Mapped to the UML Type property. It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java class. |
| Field Type Modifiers | Mapped to the specified property Type Modifier |
| Visibility Modifier | UML Property Visibility property |
| Final Modifier | UML Property Is Read Only property |
| Static Modifier | UML Property Is Static property |
| Transient Modifier | Java Language property Transient modifier |
| Volatile Modifier | Java Language property Volatile modifier |

#### TIP: Examples

Examples

Java source code

```java

```

UML model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

431872158

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c3c304e6-4ddd-43da-bf6a-f8f64a11125e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431872159</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b357fd4-5de3-46fb-981e-2270a82566ac"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431872162</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba7fe2e0-2a59-4c37-955c-99e3daefd061"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431872161</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>A Java field is mapped directly to the UML <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Attribute" /></ac:link> with the stereotype «JavaField». This stereotype is optional and if a UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java field. <span>If no appropriate property is found in UML, f</span>ield modifiers are mapped into the UML Property properties or to the Java language properties.</span></p><p><span>Note that Java field type modifiers are mapped to the specific property <em>Type Modifier</em>, but not to the UML Multiplicity. </span></p><p><strong>Field mapping table</strong></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Java Element</th><th>UML Element</th></tr><tr><td><strong>Field Declaration</strong></td><td><p><span>UML Property, owned by UML Class, with stereotype «JavaProperty» (optional)</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Field Name</strong></td><td><p><span>UML Property Name<br /></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Field Documentation</strong></td><td><p><span>UML Field Documentation</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Field Type</strong></td><td><p><span>Mapped to the UML Type property.<span><span> It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java class.</span></span><br /></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Field Type Modifiers</strong></td><td><p><span>Mapped to the specified property <em>Type Modifier</em></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Visibility Modifier</strong></td><td><p><span>UML Property <em>Visibility</em> property</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Final Modifier</strong></td><td><p><span>UML Property <em>Is Read Only</em> property</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Static Modifier</strong></td><td><p><span>UML Property <em>Is Static</em> property<br /></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Transient Modifier</strong></td><td><p><span>Java Language property <em>Transient modifier</em></span></p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>Volatile Modifier</strong></td><td colspan="1"><p><span>Java Language property <em>Volatile modifier</em></span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b4719bb-b5a6-4cf6-b295-ec204ccecd45"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Examples</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e6231941-323c-4032-b999-ec187bf31e34"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public final class MyClass
{
/**
* myList comment
*/
public static java.util.List myList;
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">UML model</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="class_with_property_ce_expample.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;"><span><br /></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d983ef41-bf29-4ab6-a917-7c32356b51ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431872158</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b92909f3-0097-4825-b226-24feefc9e5c0"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00650: Java Imports

- page_id: `189145620`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145620/Java+Imports
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:54.808+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

432040070

432040072

432040071

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Type Import | Mapped to the UML Element Import with the optional stereotype «JavaImport». The supplier is an imported UML Classifier, representing the imported Java type, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring an imported element. |
| Package Import | Mapped to the UML Package Import with the optional stereotype «JavaImport». The supplier is an imported UML Package, representing the imported Java package, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. |
| Static import for all static members | Mapped to the UML Element Import with the stereotype «JavaStaticImport». The {JavaImportAll} tag of «JavaStat- icImport» must have a “true“ value. The supplier is an imported UML Classifier owner of imported static members, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. |
| Static import for single static members | Mapped to the UML Element Import with the stereotype «JavaStaticImport». The {JavaImportAll} tag of «JavaStaticImport» must have a “false“ value and its tag {JavaImportedMember} must have reference to the imported member. The supplier is a UML Classifier owner of an imported static member, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. If several static members are imported, {JavaImportedMember} can list all of them. |

432040069

The {JavaImportAll} tag of «JavaStat- icImport» must have a “true“ value. The supplier is an imported UML Classifier owner of imported static members, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Static import for single static members</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the UML Element Import with the stereotype «JavaStaticImport». The {JavaImportAll} tag of «JavaStaticImport» must have a “false“ value and its tag {JavaImportedMember} must have reference to the imported member. The supplier is a UML Classifier owner of an imported static member, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. </span>If several static members are imported, {JavaImportedMember} can list all of them.</p></td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d7eb8ebd-beb2-4c82-9f5d-8ca10d942740"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432040069</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="8d5d7ab3-ccc4-4d7a-a867-f4d08e4f4a3c"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00651: Java Interface

- page_id: `189145607`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145607/Java+Interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:53.029+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

431916479

431916481

431916480

The Java interface is mapped directly to the UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaInterface». This stereotype is optional, and if the UML Interface has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java interface. If no appropriate property is found in the UMLinterface, modifiers are mapped into UML Interface properties or to the Java language properties for the interface. All mapping rules used in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Class' space=''] mapping apply to the Java interface.

431916478

## PAGE 00652: Java Language Options

- page_id: `189145624`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145624/Java+Language+Options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:55.234+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java CE Options
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

432105795

432105798

432105796

You can find these options at the **Options** > **Project** > **Code Engineering** > **Java Language Options**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Java language options

##### Generate Opening Bracket In New Line

By default is turned on. If element (Java type or operation) is generated for the first time into source, curly bracket is generated from the new line, if options is on, or in the same line as declaration header ends, if option is off.

##### Generate Spaces

By default it is turned on. If option is on - adds additional space after open bracket and before close bracket in parameter declaration list.

##### Generate Empty Documentation

By default is turned off. If option is turned on, the modeling tool is generating documentation to the source even, if there are no documentation in model. Just adds Java documentation start and ending symbols.

Here is generated class header with options turned on. UML Class A doesn’t have documentation, but still in code is added documentation elements.

```text
/* *
```

```text
*
```

```text
*/
```

```text
class A
```

##### Automatic “import“ Generation

By default is turned on. If option is turned on, all required imports are added automatically by used references on code generation. If this option is off, than imports are managed by user using specific mapping, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Imports' space=''].

Note, that if option is turned off, than no imports are created automatically and all imports, retrieved from source code on reverse engineering, are mapped into UML relationships as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Imports' space=''].

##### Class Count To Generate Import On Demand

By default value is 10. It means, that if there will be 9 references from the same package to different types, then imports will be generated explicitly to these classes, but if there will be 10 and more references, than it will be generated one import to the package.

This option is valid only, when “Automatic import generation“ is turned on.

##### Documentation

Java documentation has two options by itself. It has **Processor** and **Style**. Style is used to define how to format documentation by adding some comments.

There are predefined two styles:

- Style 1

```text
/* *
```

```text
*
```

```text
*/
```

- Style 2

```text
/* *
```

```text
*
```

```text
*/
```

Processor is responsible for analyzing documentation context. There are two types <none> and Java Doc. <none> options does nothing with documentation and just set it as is on element.

Java Doc is processing documentation by resolving parameter tags or on code generation building documentation by collecting comments from the UML Parameters and adding missed tags for thrown exceptions or return.

There are additional Java Doc options (button “...”). These options can be used to declare what tags would not be generated or what order to use on code generation or perhaps to add some additional tag to documentation for all elements.

Note, that Java Doc processor splits operation documentation for UML Parameter and UML Operation on source code reverse and on code generation UML Parameter documentation is used to build Java documentation for operation.

##### Classpath

You can define classpath here by referencing **jar** files or **class** files directories.

This options is used by Java reverse engineering. If referenced element is not found in model for some reasons, that it is searched in this defined path. And if class is matched by name, this class is added into model.

By default, the modeling tool imports boot classpath of the JVM, on which is running.

##### Java Source

By default is Java 5.0. There are options **1.4** and **5.0**. If you are reversing older specification source code, where, for example “**enum**“ is not a keyword and can be a variable name, then you will need to choose **1.4** Java source, else the modeling tool parser can emit error.

##### Header

It is a header for a newly generated Java files. There can be added some template string which will be preprocessed on writing to source code.

| Template Strings |  |
| --- | --- |
| $FILE_NAME | File name, without a path |
| $DATE | System date |
| $TIME | System time |
| $AUTHOR | User name on the system |

432105794

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java CE Options' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='']

Style is used to define how to format documentation by adding some comments.</p><p>There are predefined two styles:</p><ul><li>Style 1</li></ul><pre>/* * </pre><pre> *</pre><pre> */</pre><ul><li>Style 2</li></ul><pre> /* * </pre><pre> * </pre><pre> */</pre><p>Processor is responsible for analyzing documentation context. There are two types &lt;none&gt; and Java Doc. &lt;none&gt; options does nothing with documentation and just set it as is on element.</p><p>Java Doc is processing documentation by resolving parameter tags or on code generation building documentation by collecting comments from the UML Parameters and adding missed tags for thrown exceptions or return.</p><p>There are additional Java Doc options (button “...”). These options can be used to declare what tags would not be generated or what order to use on code generation or perhaps to add some additional tag to documentation for all elements.</p><p>Note, that Java Doc processor splits operation documentation for UML Parameter and UML Operation on source code reverse and on code generation UML Parameter documentation is used to build Java documentation for operation.</p><h3>Classpath</h3><p>You can define classpath here by referencing <strong>jar</strong> files or <strong>class</strong> files directories.</p><p>This options is used by Java reverse engineering. There can be added some template string which will be preprocessed on writing to source code.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 40.853657%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 51.870323%;" /><col style="width: 48.129677%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="2">Template Strings</th></tr><tr><td>$FILE_NAME</td><td>File name, without a path</td></tr><tr><td>$DATE</td><td>System date</td></tr><tr><td>$TIME</td><td>System time</td></tr><tr><td>$AUTHOR</td><td>User name on the system</td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aec5c50e-2b42-479c-93e8-b40bbfdae4b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432105794</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="bcdd00b0-7443-40e7-87e0-5df0a2ae412e"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b9ac038b-25f4-4616-930b-ca7bcfce08b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145593 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00653: Java Mapping to UML

- page_id: `189145593`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145593/Java+Mapping+to+UML
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:50.813+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering
- labels: ['java-profile']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1295645483

1295645486

1295645484

##### Java Profile

Java Profile is used to map the Java language to UML.

UML specification does not provide elements to fully cover JLS; thus, a modeling tool uses UML stereotypes to mark a UML class or interface to be a specific Java element.

Java stereotypes are provided in the *Java_Profile.xml file* in the profiles directory. Some stereotypes have tagged values used for mapping special Java language elements or keywords that are not mapped to standard UML properties. All Java stereotypes are derived from the «JavaElement». Another abstract stereotype, «JavaTypeElement», is used to group all Java type elements. These two stereotypes are abstract and are not used directly.

Each stereotype is used to represent an appropriate Java element:

- «JavaImport» represents [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Imports' space=''] ,
- «JavaOperation» represents [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Operation' space=''] , and
- «JavaClass» represents [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Class' space=''] type.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Stereotypes.

1295645482

**Related Pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="19486c04-31ed-43a3-ae0a-349d59bf090c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295645483</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f86640b5-2030-42d7-ba6c-03de21a0da06"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295645486</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="18817f27-846f-4696-9223-873c2afac6dc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295645484</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3>Java Profile</h3><p>Java Profile is used to map the Java language to UML.</p><p>UML specification does not provide elements to fully cover JLS; thus, a modeling tool uses UML stereotypes to mark a UML class or interface to be a specific Java element.</p><p>Java stereotypes are provided in the <em>Java_Profile.xml file</em> in the profiles directory. Some stereotypes have tagged values used for mapping special Java language elements or keywords that are not mapped to standard UML properties. All Java stereotypes are derived from the «JavaElement». Another abstract stereotype, «JavaTypeElement», is used to group all Java type elements. These two stereotypes are abstract and are not used directly.</p><p>Each stereotype is used to represent an appropriate Java element:</p><ul><li>«JavaImport» represents <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java Imports" /></ac:link>,</li><li>«JavaOperation» represents <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java Operation" /></ac:link>, and </li><li>«JavaClass» represents <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java Class" /></ac:link> type.</li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="stereotype_structure_ce.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Stereotypes.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ebdcffba-83e4-4cfc-8e31-81cb405f9b98"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295645482</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="cf49da9d-a381-49e7-918b-7c2d824997da" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="1b9c82d4-5c26-4bdd-a7a4-aa7a57c7a60d"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145605 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00654: Java Operation

- page_id: `189145605`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145605/Java+Operation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:52.822+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Examples

431890768

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Examples

431890770

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Examples

431890769

The Java Operation mapped directly to the UML Operation is done with the stereotype «JavaOperation». This stereotype is optional, so if the UML class has no stereotype Java CE treats it as a Java Operation. If no appropriate property is found in UMLOperation, modifiers are mapped into UML Property properties or to the Java language properties. The Java Operation return type is mapped to the UML Type property of the UML Parameter with the “Return“ direction kind. Java Operation parameters are mapped to the UML Parameters. The direction kind is set “In“ for “final” parameters. Note that Java parameter type modifiers are mapped to the UML specific property “Type Modifier,“ but not to the UML Multiplicity.

**Operation mapping table**

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Operation declaration | UML Operation, owned by UML Class, with stereotype «JavaOperation» (optional) |
| Operation Name | UML Operation Name |
| Operation documentation | UML Operation Documentation |
| Parameters list | UML Operation “Parameters” list |
| Return type | Mapped to the UML Parameter type with “return“ direction kind, i.e., it resides in the UML Operation parameters list. It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java class |
| Return type modifiers | Mapped to the modeling tool specified property “Type Modifier“ |
| Visibility modifier | UML Operation “Visibility” property |
| Final modifier | UML Operation “Is Leaf“ property |
| Abstract modifier | UML Operation “Is Abstract“ property |
| Static modifier | UML Operation “Is Static“ property |
| Synchronized modifier | UML Operation “Concurrency“ kind “guarded” |
| Throws list | UML Operation “Raised Exception“ list. It is a list of references to the UML Classes; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java exception class. |
| Native modifier | Java Language property “Native modifier” |
| Strictfp modifier | Java Language property “Strictfp modifier” |

**Parameter mapping table**

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Parameter declaration | UML Parameter, owned by UML Operation, with stereotype «JavaParameter» (optional) |
| Parameter name | UML Parameter Name |
| Parameter documentation | When used in JavaDoc preprocessing, it is mapped to the UML Parameter Documentation. If not, it is part of the UML Operation Documentation. |
| Return type | Mapped to the UML Type property. It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java exception class. |
| Return type modifiers | Mapped to the modeling tool specified property “Type Modifier“ |
| Final modifier | Direction kind “in“ of the UML Parameter |


## PAGE 00655: Java Package

- page_id: `189145599`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145599/Java+Package
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:52.140+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

431568590

431568592

431568591

A Java package is mapped to the general UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='']. It does not have any specific [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='']and properties. However, if the UML package represents the Java package, it must not have a «modelLibrary» stereotype from a standard UML profile. The «modelLibrary» stereotype is used to show the root package, where the Java package tree ends and no parent packages, including «modelLibrary», are part of the Java package structure. The figurebelow shows *java.lang.String* added into the *working package*. Packages *java* and *lang* represent general Java packages, but *working package* with *L* is stereotyped with «modelLibrary» and is not part of the Java package.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Package Structure

431568589

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

It does not have any specific <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[stereotypes ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>and properties. However, if the UML package represents the Java package, it must not have a «modelLibrary» stereotype from a standard UML profile. The «modelLibrary» stereotype is used to show the root package, where the Java package tree ends and no parent packages, including «modelLibrary», are part of the Java package structure. The figure </span>below shows <em>java.lang.String</em> added into the <em>working package</em>. Packages <em>java</em> and <em>lang</em> represent general Java packages, but <em>working package</em> with <em>L</em> is stereotyped with «modelLibrary» and is not part of the Java package.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="package_structure.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Package Structure</span></h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1a3974c8-6575-4cb5-a773-79f783a1bbfa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431568589</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="edec066a-1303-467e-9645-e3305d60f4b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145619 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00656: Java Parameterized Type

- page_id: `189145619`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145619/Java+Parameterized+Type
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:54.618+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

432024497

432024500

432024498

Parameterized types are mapped to the general UML Classifier connected with a UML Template Binding to the UML Classifier. The supplier of this binding link is the UML Classifier with UML Template Parameters and represents the Java generic type with type parameters. The client of the UML Template Binding is the UML Classifier of the same UML type as the supplier. Java type parameters are mapped directly to the UML Template Parameter Substitution of the UML Template Binding.

Parameterized type mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Parametrized type | This is called bounded elements in UML, and is connected to the UML Classifier by the UML Template Binding as a client. The client must be of the same type as the supplier. The supplier must have at least one UML Template Parameter. |
| Parameter for type | Mapped to the UML Template Parameter Substitution of the UML Template Binding. Each UML Template Parameter from the supplier must be substituted by the UML Template Parameter Substitution. The type of the type parameter is any reference to the UML Classifier from the model set as “Actual“ value of the UML Template Parameter Substitution. |
| Type modifiers of the parameter | Mapped to the property “Type modifiers” of the UML Template Parameter Substitution |
| Wildcard | A UML Class with the name “?“ from the Java Profile is used as an “Actual” value in the UML Template Parameter Substitution. |
| Wildcard with bounds | Java bounding type is mapped to the UML Classifier and used as the “Actual” value in UML Template Parameter Substitution. “? extends“ or “? super“ bounding is mapped to the appropriate tag {JavaArgumentBount} value “extends“ or “super“ of the «JavaTypeArgument» stereotype applied to the UML Template Parameter Substitution. |

**Example**

When you have a type with type variables represented in the model, you can create a parameterized type for Java. For this you must create an empty UML Classifier of the same type as the template classifier (type with type variables). Then create a UML Template Binding and create a UML Template Parameter Substitution for the UML Template Parameters.

To create a “java.util.List<String>” type, you must create a UML Interface first, with a UML Template Parameter representing “java.util.List”.

Then create another UML Interface and connect it with the List interface with UML Template Binding.

Note that to draw a UML Template Binding, the client element must have at least one UML Template Parameter created.

You must open the UML Template Binding specification dialog and create a UML Template Parameter Substitution for the appropriate UML Template Parameter.

For a created substitution element, you need to select actual value. In the current example, it is a UML class representing “java.lang.String”.

Now we have created a parameterized type, which can be used in the model to represent type java.util.List<String> parameters.

432024496

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145595 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00657: Java Referenced Types

- page_id: `189145595`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145595/Java+Referenced+Types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:51.089+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431453737

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431453740

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431453739

Java built-in types are used from the UML Standard Profile, which is automatically loaded with every new project.

#### INFO: Show Auxiliary Resources

Show Auxiliary Resources

The UML Standard Profile by default is hidden. If you want to see it, select**Show Auxiliary Resources**in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Every referenced class from the other libraries (including JDK libraries) should be imported/created into a project and referenced in the CES reference path (by default the reference path is *Model* package in the model).

A modeling tool resolves referenced classes from the specified class path (by default, the class path is the boot class path taken from the JVM on which the modeling tool is started). It creates an appropriate package structure as described below in **Sorting reversed classes according to the classpath**.

If a referenced class is not found in the model or the class path, the reference is created in the *Default* package.

Java Profile defines the UML Class with the name "?". It is used for mapping parameterized types.

**Sorting reversed classes according to the classpath**

References are sorted to packages according to the classpath entry.

The figure below shows how the reverse result of the following java code is represented:

#### TIP: Example

java

Example

Java code

```java

```

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Sample: The String, JFrame and CustomFrame classes location after reverse

431453736

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Mapping to UML' space='']

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="27ea4511-f2b0-4d20-8281-51cd3b660f43"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431453737</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72d0b01b-1df5-4ce5-ba36-8d0bffd5255d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431453740</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fbea88d3-963c-4865-b70b-27aff7f67b38"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431453739</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java built-in types are used from the UML Standard Profile, which is automatically loaded with every new project.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1603b460-2579-46a5-85b4-d67766ced720"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Show Auxiliary Resources</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>The UML Standard Profile by default is hidden. If you want to see it, select </span><strong>Show Auxiliary Resources </strong><span>in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link>.</span></p><p><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_auxiliary_resources.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>Every referenced class from the other libraries (including JDK libraries) should be imported/created into a project and referenced in the CES reference path (by default the reference path is <em>Model</em> package in the model).</span></p><p>A modeling tool resolves referenced classes from the specified class path (by default, the class path is the boot class path taken from the JVM on which the modeling tool is started). It creates an appropriate package structure as described below in <strong>Sorting reversed classes according to the classpath</strong>.</p><p>If a referenced class is not found in the model or the class path, the reference is created in the <em>Default</em> package. </p><p>Java Profile defines the UML Class with the name &quot;?&quot;. It is used for mapping parameterized types.</p><p><br /></p><p><strong>Sorting reversed classes according to the classpath</strong></p><p>References are sorted to packages according to the classpath entry.</p><p>The figure below shows how the reverse result of the following java code is represented:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cbadf13a-4e59-4c20-b1ec-295d38e60b7f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Java code</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c77c7c5d-0647-466f-8363-cce135551cae"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import javax.swing.*; public class CustomFrame extends JFrame { String mTitle; }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sample_classes_location_after_reverse.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Sample: The String, JFrame and CustomFrame classes location after reverse </h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c764f472-8207-4402-b69d-704c7207bc5e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431453736</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="3778e456-3d8f-4011-b77e-3cdfd7c048f9"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java Mapping to UML" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145618 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00658: Java Type Variables

- page_id: `189145618`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145618/Java+Type+Variables
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:54.389+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

432009432

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

432009435

INLINE

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

432009433

INLINE

Type variables are mapped to the UML Template Parameter of a UML Class, Interface or UML Operation, according to which Java element has type variables. If bound types are present, they are mapped into the UML Class or UML Interface connected with the UML Generalization or UML Interface Realization as a supplier. The client is a UML Class, which is a “Parametered Element” of the UML Template Parameter.

Type variable mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Type variable declaration | Mapped to the UML Template Parameter. This UML Template Parameter is of the Class type from the UML Metamodel. The UML Template Parameter has the property “Parametered Element“ of the UML Template Parameter. The metamodel type “Class“ is taken from the “UML Standard profile/UML 2 Metamodel” |
| Type variable name | Mapped to the UML Class name. This UML Class is “Parametered Element“ |
| Type bounds | If a bound type is a Java class, it is mapped to the General class of the “Parametered Element“. If a bound type is a Java interface, than it is mapped to the Realized Interface. |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code****

```java
{ 
    E attribute; 
}]]>
```

In this example, the Template Parameter “E” is a subtype of the Cloneable class (e.g. “java.util.Cloneable“). “E” is used to type the “Test” public class attribute.

432009431

INLINE

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

If bound types are present, they are mapped into the UML Class or UML Interface connected with the UML Generalization or UML Interface Realization as a supplier. The client is a UML Class, which is a “Parametered Element” of the UML Template Parameter. </span></p><p><br /></p><p>Type variable mapping table</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Java Element</th><th>UML element</th></tr><tr><td>Type variable declaration</td><td>Mapped to the UML Template Parameter.     E attribute;

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189144924 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00659: Javadoc syntax

- page_id: `189144924`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189144924/Javadoc+syntax
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:21.641+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Javadoc syntax tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A doc comment consists of characters between the characters /** that begin the comment and the characters */ that end it. Leading asterisks are allowed on each line and are described below. Text in a comment can continue to multiple lines.

- Leading asterisks When Javadoc Tool parses a doc comment, leading asterisk (*) characters on each line are discarded.
- First sentence The first sentence of each doc comment is a summary sentence. This sentence can be retrieved from $doc.firstSentenceTags. The first sentence tags are a collection of inline tags until full stops (.).
- Comment and tag sections A comment is the main description which begins after the starting delimiters /** and continues until the tag section. A tag section starts with the first block tag. The comment can be retrieved by $doc.comment
- Comments written in HTML Text is written in HTML and will be rendered as HTML support before being printed to a report. (See Appendix D: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Appendix D: HTML tag support' space='MD2024xR2'] )
- **Block and in-line tags** 
A tag is a special keyword within a doc comment that Javadoc Tool can process. There are two kinds of tags: 
**(i) Block tags** 
 They can be placed only in the tag section. The block tag form is @tag. 
 The block tag can be accessed directly from the root document such as $doc.param and $doc.author. 
 The $doc.tags will provide a collection of all the tags appearing in this Javadoc. **(ii) Inline tags** 
 They can be placed anywhere in the main description or in the block tags. The inline tag form is {@tag}.

The table below lists Current Supported Tags

| Tag | JDK | Report Wizard Support |
| --- | --- | --- |
| @author | 1.0 | Yes |
| {@code} | 1.5 | Render as <code>text</code> |
| {@docRoot} | 1.3 | Not supported |
| @deprecated | 1.0 | Yes |
| @exception | 1.0 | Yes |
| {@inheritDoc} | 1.4 | Not supported |
| {@link} | 1.2 | Yes, with conditions (External link, model support, and class/method link will be ignored) |
| {@linkplain} | 1.4 | Yes, see {@link link} |
| {@literal} | 1.5 | Yes |
| @param | 1.0 | Yes |
| @return | 1.0 | Yes |
| @see | 1.0 | Yes, with conditions (External link, model support, and class/method link will be return as plain text) |
| @serial | 1.2 | Yes |
| @serialData | 1.2 | Yes |
| @serialField | 1.2 | Yes |
| @since | 1.1 | Yes |
| @throws | 1.2 | Yes |
| {@value} | 1.4 | Not Supported |
| @version | 1.0 | Yes |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Text in a comment can continue to multiple lines.</p><ul><li><strong>Leading asterisks</strong><br />When Javadoc Tool parses a doc comment, leading asterisk (*) characters on each line are discarded.</li><li><strong>First sentence</strong><br />The first sentence of each doc comment is a summary sentence. <br />This sentence can be retrieved from $doc.firstSentenceTags. <br />The first sentence tags are a collection of inline tags until full stops (.).</li><li><strong>Comment and tag sections</strong><br />A comment is the main description which begins after the starting delimiters /** and <br />continues until the tag section. A tag section starts with the first block tag. The  comment can be retrieved by $doc.comment </li><li><strong>Comments written in HTML</strong><br />Text is written in HTML and will be rendered as HTML support before being printed to a report. (See Appendix D: <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Appendix D: HTML tag support" ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[HTML Tag Support]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>)</li><li><p><strong>Block and in-line tags</strong><br />A tag is a special keyword within a doc comment that Javadoc Tool can process. There are two kinds of tags:<br /><strong>     (i) Block tags</strong><br />         They can be placed only in the tag section. The block tag form is @tag. <br />         The block tag can be accessed directly from the root document such as $doc.param and        $doc.author. <br />         The $doc.tags will provide a collection of all the tags appearing in this Javadoc.</p><p> <strong>    (ii) Inline tags</strong><br />          They can be placed anywhere in the main description or in the block tags. The inline tag form is {@tag}.</p></li></ul><p>The table below lists Current Supported Tags</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 38.2806%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.8092%;" /><col style="width: 9.44123%;" /><col style="width: 69.8683%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Tag</th><th>JDK</th><th>Report Wizard supported</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@deprecated</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@exception</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@inheritDoc}</td><td colspan="1">1.4</td><td colspan="1">Not supported</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@link}</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td colspan="1">Yes, with conditions <br />(External link, model support, <br />and class/method link will be <br />ignored)</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@linkplain}</td><td colspan="1">1.4</td><td colspan="1">Yes, see <br />{@link link}</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@literal}</td><td colspan="1">1.5</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@param</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@return</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@see</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes, with conditions <br />(External link, model <br />support, and class/method <br />link will be return as plain text)</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@serial</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td
