10GB, 10GB, 36GB</li><li>Converting between UAF, DoDAF2, DoDAF, MODAF, NAF, NAF4 frameworks - 8GB, 9GB, 13GB</li><li>Project Merge - 11GB, 13GB, 21GB <sup>3</sup></li></ul><p><sup>3 </sup>strongly depends on the amount of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the specified required memory is for merging projects with 1.5k changes in total).</p><h3>Disk Space</h3><p>The recommended disk space for installation and configuration files is 3GB or more depending on used plugins, project type (local or server), and project size. In addition to this, large server projects require extra disk space for configuration files: </p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="2">Project Elements Count<br />(including used projects)</th><th style="text-align: center;" rowspan="2">Disk Space</th></tr><tr><th style="text-align: center;">19.0</th><th style="text-align: center;">2021x</th></tr><tr><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">1.5 million</td><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">1 million</td><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">6GB</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: center;">2.5 million</td><td style="text-align: center;">2 million</td><td style="text-align: center;">10GB</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">5 million</td><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">3.5 million</td><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">30GB</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br />The amount of required disk space proportionally increases if working on multiple projects.</p><p><strong>Note:</strong> Element History and Content History features usage requires significantly more disk space due to intensive caching at the client side.</p><p>Depending on the feature usage scenario (e.g., retrieving content history from old/distant project versions multiple times), the disk space might increase by up to 100GB.</p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189139687 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00720: Memory monitor

- page_id: `189139687`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189139687/Memory+monitor
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:18.342+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Getting started > Improving performance
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Running out of memory

1427409680

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Running out of memory

1427409682

238600894

**On this page:**

3

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Running out of memory

1427409681

When working with larger models, turn on the Memory Monitor to monitor the memory used by a modeling tool. The Memory Monitor shows currently used memory and the current Java heap size. Typically, when you start working with a model, used memory increases because actions are stored in the undo list, and opened diagrams are loaded.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The memory monitor bar showing currently used memory and the current Java heap size.

##### Turning on the Memory Monitor

To turn on the Memory Monitor

- In the main menu of a modeling tool, select View > Status Line > Show Memory Monitor . The Memory Monitor bar appears in the bottom-right corner of the modeling tool window.

#### WARNING: Running out of memory

Running out of memory

When used memory takes more than 85% of the total heap size, the Memory Monitor bar turns red, indicating that a modeling tool may run out of memory soon.

##### Freeing unused memory with the Garbage Collector

If a modeling tool uses all the Java heap size, you can try to recover enough memory for saving a project with the Garbage Collector, but this may take some time.

To free unused memory with the Garbage Collector

- Click anywhere on the Memory Monitor bar and select Run Garbage Collector .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Memory Monitor pop-up window

If you find it impossible to use the modeling tool and/or see the Out of Memory error, you should dump the memory heap to the**.hprof**file that is created in the defined location on the computer and then submit this file to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Support_obsolete' space='NMDOC'] for further assistance regarding the memory consumption issue.

##### Dumping the memory heap

To dump the memory heap

Do one of the following:

- Click anywhere on the Memory Monitor bar, select Dump Memory Heap , and then set the desired name and location of the memory heap file. By default, the name of the file is generated according to the current timestamp.
- Press Ctrl+Shift+H on your keyboard and then define the name and location of the generated memory heap file.

You must create a new file for the heap dump since the memory heap cannot be dumped into an existing file.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Dumping memory heap

If the Memory Monitor bar is still red, save the model, restart the modeling tool, and reload the model. If the Memory Monitor bar stays red after reloading the model, you should [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Memory allocation' space=''].

##### Dumping the memory histogram

To dump the memory histogram

Do one of the following:

- Click anywhere on the Memory Monitor bar, select Dump Memory Histogram , and then set the desired name and location of the memory histogram file. By default, the name of the file is generated according to the current timestamp.
- Press Ctrl+Alt+Shift+H on your keyboard and then define the name and location of the generated memory histogram file. To use the **Dump Memory Histogram** functionality, the application must run with the JDK or JRE that comes with the installation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Dumping memory histogram

1427409679

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Improving performance' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Background Task Manager' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Memory allocation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Active validation period' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7b869093-0a5e-41af-adce-166db0578523"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1427409680</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="870ce3c8-87d9-4354-a258-5500457ebb79"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1427409682</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8da67a79-d0d3-4e20-8bf5-55bb48c2d596"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">238600894</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page:</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94883e87-d5c5-4850-85b5-48192b30e303"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d29d87d9-9ce4-4ea3-aecd-f0843ba43a8a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1427409681</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When working with larger models, turn on the Memory Monitor to monitor the memory used by a modeling tool. The Memory Monitor shows currently used memory and the current Java heap size. Typically, when you start working with a model, used memory increases because actions are stored in the undo list, and opened diagrams are loaded.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="memory_monitoring.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The memory monitor bar showing currently used memory and the current Java heap size.</h6><h3>Turning on the Memory Monitor</h3><p>To turn on the Memory Monitor</p><hr /><ul><li>In the main menu of a modeling tool, select <strong>View</strong> &gt; <strong>Status Line</strong> &gt; <strong>Show Memory Monitor</strong>. The Memory Monitor bar appears in the bottom-right corner of the modeling tool window.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d48978ce-5014-42b7-8c1d-3328ec4d0baf"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Running out of memory</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When used memory takes more than 85% of the total heap size, the Memory Monitor bar turns red, indicating that a modeling tool may run out of memory soon.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span style="font-size: 16.0px;font-weight: bold;letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Freeing unused memory with the Garbage Collector</span></h3><p>If a modeling tool uses all the Java heap size, you can try to recover enough memory for saving a project with the Garbage Collector, but this may take some time.</p><p><br />To free unused memory with the Garbage Collector</p><hr /><ul><li>Click anywhere on the Memory Monitor bar and select <strong>Run Garbage Collector</strong>. <br /><br /></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="run_garbage_collector.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Memory Monitor pop-up window</h6><p><br /></p><p>If you find it impossible to use the modeling tool and/or see the Out of Memory error, you should dump the memory heap to the<strong> .hprof </strong>file that is created in the defined location on the computer and then submit this file to <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Support_obsolete" ri:space-key="NMDOC" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[customer support]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for further assistance regarding the memory consumption issue. </p><h3>Dumping the memory heap</h3><p>To dump the memory heap</p><hr /><p>Do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Click anywhere on the Memory Monitor bar, select <strong>Dump Memory Heap</strong>, and then set the desired name and location of the memory heap file. By default, the name of the file is generated according to the current timestamp. </li><li>Press Ctrl+Shift+H on your keyboard and then define the name and location of the generated memory heap file. </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="514b3a97-9dbd-4b7c-94ef-e8f13e73bb5e"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You must create a new file for the heap dump since the memory heap cannot be dumped into an existing file.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dump_memory_heap.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Dumping memory heap</h6><p>If the Memory Monitor bar is still red, save the model, restart the modeling tool, and reload the model. If the Memory Monitor bar stays red after reloading the model, you should <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Memory allocation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[increase the maximum Java heap size]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h3>Dumping the memory histogram</h3><p>To dump the memory histogram</p><hr /><p>Do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Click anywhere on the Memory Monitor bar, select <strong>Dump Memory Histogram</strong>, and then set the desired name and location of the memory histogram file. By default, the name of the file is generated according to the current timestamp. </li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Press Ctrl+Alt+Shift+H on your keyboard and then define the name and location of the generated memory histogram file. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="03cb561f-6337-499d-8d23-5cac3e0ded5e"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To use the <strong>Dump Memory Histogram</strong> functionality, the application must run with the JDK or JRE that comes with the installation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dump_memory_histogram.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Dumping memory histogram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="628e74f3-7e58-465a-bdb6-53f96626f15a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1427409679</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Improving performance" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Background Task Manager" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Memory allocation" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Active validation period" /></ac:link></li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145515 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00721: Merging property groups and subgroups

- page_id: `189145515`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145515/Merging+property+groups+and+subgroups
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:44.999+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Creating Property Groups and Subgroups
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can specify property groups and subgroups for different customization elements of the same element. That element will have groups and subgroups from all customization elements.

#### INFO: Example

Example

Let’s say we have two customizations of the same element type, the actor. The first customization has the property group *My Group* with the subgroup *Subgroup 1* containing the property *Prop 1*. The second customization has the same property group with the same subgroup specified, but the subgroup *Subgroup 1* contains the property *Prop 2* .

As a result, the actor element type will have *My Group,* with *Subgroup 1* containing both*Prop 1* and *Prop 2*.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Property Groups and Subgroups' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>You can specify property groups and subgroups for different customization elements of the same element. That element will have groups and subgroups from all customization elements. </span></p><p><span><br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d70bdea3-8b22-4fee-9042-256bf3a1707e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>Let’s say we have two customizations of the same element type, the actor. The first customization has the property group </span> <em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="63646f21-6f23-4d63-a774-0eed08769f53">My Group</ac:inline-comment-marker> </em> <span>with the subgroup </span> <span><em>Subgroup 1</em> </span> <span>containing the property </span> <em>Prop 1 </em><span>. The second customization has the same property group with the same subgroup specified, but the subgroup </span> <em>Subgroup 1 </em> <span>contains the property </span> <em>Prop 2</em> <span>. </span></p><p>As a result, the actor element type will have <em>My Group,</em> with <em>Subgroup 1</em> containing both<em> Prop 1</em> and <em>Prop 2</em>. </p></div></div></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span> <br /> </span></p></div></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0e78ddfd-bec9-4837-bd51-242520587f8a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Customization Data" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Customization Data" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Property Groups and Subgroups" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141547 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00722: Message

- page_id: `189141547`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141547/Message
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:20.768+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: ['synchcall', 'asynchcall', 'asynchsignal', 'reply', 'createmessage', 'deletemessage', 'message-syntax', 'message-sort']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

372179598

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

372179600

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

372179599

#### EXCERPT: Example

INLINE

A Message defines a particular communication between [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lifeline' space=''] of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Use' space=''], such as raising a Signal, invoking an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operation' space=''], or creating or destroying an Instance. Messages specify the kind of communication, the sender, and the receiver.

A Message is represented by arrows between the lifelines. The style of the Message line and arrowhead reflect the types of the Message.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can see the description of a selected property in the description area of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']. To see descriptions, be sure the **Show Description** option is selected.

##### Message sorts

| Message Sort | Description | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| synchCall | The Message was generated by a synchronous call to an Operation. Synchronous Messages can overtake each other. When a Message represents an Operation, the arguments of the Message must correspond to the parameters of the Operation. |  |
| asynchCall | Asynchronous means that the caller continues immediately after the call. asynchCall Messages do not have reply Messages. |  |
| asynchSignal | The Message was generated by an asynchronous send Action. The argument of the Message must correspond to the attributes of the Signal. |  |
| reply | A reply Message to an Operation call. |  |
| createMessage | The Message designates the creation of another Lifeline object. No other Messages on a given Lifeline in an Interaction operand may appear above a Lifeline. |  |
| deleteMessage | The Message designates the termination of another Lifeline. |  |

##### Message syntax

The Message name is displayed on the message path on the diagram pane. The syntax of the message name is as follows:

*<messageident> ::= [<attribute> '='] <signal or operation name> ['(' [<argument> [','*

*<argument>]* ')'] [':' <return value>]*

*<argument> ::= ([<parameter name> '='] <argument-value>) / '-'*

#### TIP: Example

Example

*v=fo(P1="a", P2=True):12*

****

**Common actions with Messages**

To set an action type for a Message

- In the Message [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] , click the Message Sort property value cell and select the action type from the drop-down list.
- On the diagram pane, right-click the Message and select a desired action type from the shortcut menu.

To show / hide Message numbers

1. Right-click the diagram pane to open its shortcut menu.
2. Then do one of the following:

- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space=''] or Time diagram , select / clear Show Message Numbers .
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communication diagram' space=''] , select / clear Numbering > Show Message Numbers .

372179596

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lifeline' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Use' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="822ba8cc-c0ec-4d97-8f11-8258e93001ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">372179598</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b139e57a-2206-4ce5-aee2-a45e8fa1106e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">372179600</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36cf709c-1a3e-4970-b217-d30ba5559c34"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">372179599</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06e751a1-0bf7-4bf6-8901-22b44fe37360"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>A Message defines a particular communication between <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Lifeline" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Lifelines]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interaction Use" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interaction]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, such as raising a Signal, invoking an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operation" /></ac:link>, or creating or destroying an Instance. Messages specify the kind of communication, the sender, and the receiver.</p><p>A Message is represented by arrows between the lifelines. The style of the Message line and arrowhead reflect the types of the Message.</p><p><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:title="Example of Message in Sequence diagram" ac:alt="Example of Message in Sequence diagram" ac:height="192" ac:width="292"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_message_sequence_diagram.png" /></ac:image>    <ac:image ac:border="true" ac:title="Example of Message in Communication diagram" ac:alt="Example of Message in Communication diagram" ac:height="230" ac:width="367"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_message_communication_diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br />You can see the description of a selected property in the description area of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. To see descriptions, be sure the <strong>Show Description</strong> option is selected.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Message sorts</span></h3><div class="WordSection3"><p><br /></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup> <col /> <col /> <col /> </colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p>Message Sort</p></th><th>Description</th><th>Example</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1">synchCall</td><td colspan="1"><p>The Message was generated by a synchronous call to an Operation. Synchronous Messages can overtake each other. When a Message represents an Operation, the arguments of the Message must correspond to the parameters of the Operation.</p></td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="105" ac:width="230"><ri:attachment ri:filename="synchCall_message.png" /></ac:image></div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">asynchCall</td><td colspan="1">Asynchronous means that the caller continues immediately after the call. asynchCall Messages do not have reply Messages.</td><td rowspan="2"><div class="content-wrapper"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="109" ac:width="291"><ri:attachment ri:filename="asynchCall_message.png" /></ac:image></div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">asynchSignal</td><td colspan="1">The Message was generated by an asynchronous send Action. The argument of the Message must correspond to the attributes of the Signal.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">reply</td><td colspan="1">A reply Message to an Operation call.</td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="127" ac:width="226"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reply_message.png" /></ac:image></div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">createMessage</td><td colspan="1">The Message designates the creation of another Lifeline object. No other Messages on a given Lifeline in an Interaction operand may appear above a Lifeline.</td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="149" ac:width="281"><ri:attachment ri:filename="createMessage.png" /></ac:image></div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">deleteMessage</td><td colspan="1">The Message designates the termination of another Lifeline.</td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="135" ac:width="201"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deleteMessage.png" /></ac:image></div></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div><div class="WordSection1"><h3>Message syntax</h3><p>The Message name is displayed on the message path on the diagram pane. The syntax of the message name is as follows:</p><p><em>&lt;messageident&gt; ::= [&lt;attribute&gt; '='] &lt;signal or operation name&gt; ['(' [&lt;argument&gt; [','</em></p><p><em>&lt;argument&gt;]* ')'] [':' &lt;return value&gt;]</em></p><p><em>&lt;argument&gt; ::= ([&lt;parameter name&gt; '='] &lt;argument-value&gt;) / '-'</em></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="32eca1a1-94ec-4a40-9b52-7326980fbe2d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p> <em>v=fo(P1=&quot;a&quot;, P2=True):12</em></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 16.0px;line-height: 1.5;"> <br /></strong></p><p><strong style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 16.0px;line-height: 1.5;">Common actions with Messages</strong></p><p>To set an action type for a Message</p><hr />Do one of the following:<br /><ul><li>In the Message <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, click the <strong>Message Sort</strong> property value cell and select the action type from the drop-down list.</li><li>On the diagram pane, right-click the Message and select a desired action type from the shortcut menu.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To show / hide Message numbers</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the diagram pane to open its shortcut menu.</li><li>Then do one of the following:</li></ol><ul><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Sequence ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>or <span class="confluence-link">Time diagram</span>, select / clear <strong>Show Message Numbers</strong>.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Communication diagram" /></ac:link>, select / clear <strong>Numbering</strong> &gt; <strong>Show Message Numbers</strong>.</li></ul></div></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c4de03a6-4eaa-4069-9eb0-ec522c4f826a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">372179596</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="73f9a815-d600-41a3-8baf-9069d61d4884" /></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification Window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Lifeline" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Lifelines]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interaction Use" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interaction]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````
